content.text:
## Role

- You are a subagent implementing exactly one issue in a larger plan. Stay strictly within its scope.

## Work item context

## Orientation (why this issue exists — not work to do)
- Project goal: Migrate integrations-core off the requests library onto httpx via an HTTP-agnostic protocol and shared exception library.
- Issue phase/group: Phase 3 — Repo agnosticization (vertical slices) (HTTPX-G3)
- Phase description: **Goal:** remove every `requests`-specific reference from shipped **integration code and its tests** (~50 integrations), leaving each integration on the agnostic surface only. Backend stays `requests`.

**Decomposition — vertical slices, not horizontal layers.** Following PR #24310 (base translation + integration narrowing + those integrations' test updates in one unit), each issue owns a **batch of integrations and fixes all of their `requests` references at once** — production catch-narrowing, session/auth/factory adoption, and the paired test decoupling, plus per-integration `changelog.d/<PR>.fixed`. This keeps every issue self-contained and green, matches the shipped workflow, and avoids R2 serializing a separate "production" and "test" issue that touch the same integration's files.

**Per-slice moves (each integration takes the subset that applies):**
- Narrow `self.http` catch sites to agnostic-only (~47 sites; no dual-catch). Swap bare `raise requests...` (nifi); reparent elastic `AuthenticationError`; drop the cloud_foundry_api dual-arm.
- Replace `self.http.session.*`/`_session` accessors (~5: cisco_aci, http_check, avi_vantage) with the Phase 2 capabilities.
- Migrate `AuthBase` subclasses (voltdb) to the agnostic auth hook.
- Reroute bespoke `RequestsWrapper(...)` (kube_*, hpe_aruba_edgeconnect) through the Phase 2 factory.
- Route bare `requests.get/post` (legacy openstack) through the protocol.
- In the same slice: move that integration's `requests.Session` test patches to `mock_http`/OM fixtures, replace test-built `requests.Response` with `MockHTTPResponse`, and swap `requests.exceptions` test imports/asserts to agnostic.
- One cross-cutting slice: retire the `datadog_checks_dev` `MockResponse` and migrate its consumers (argocd, bentoml, kubernetes_state, marklogic, prefect, sonatype_nexus, traefik_mesh, vsphere, +base ref) together.

**Batching:** group by mechanical class / subsystem / CODEOWNERS so each batch is reviewable and collision-free; slices are independent and run in parallel once their Phase 2 dependency (if any) is met. The catch-narrowing slices carry the exception-translation dependency; slices touching only session/auth/factory carry the matching capability dependency. Harness HTTP (dd_environment, WaitFor, fixture-seeding, ~28 integrations) is **exempt** — the grep gate is interception-only. Assert observable behavior only; never call counts/URLs. Any latent bug found is split out to master separately (per the "Changes to Split Out" page), not fixed here.

**Validation (high level):** each slice's integrations pass `ddev test`, and a committed grep asserts those integrations carry no `requests`-specific reference in production or interception-path tests.

Work item: HTTPX-13 — Retire the requests-based dev MockResponse and migrate remaining consumers

## Intent
Delete the requests-based MockResponse from datadog_checks_dev once every consumer is off it, so the shared test helper no longer anchors requests in the dev toolkit and the agnostic MockHTTPResponse is the single test double.

## Description
Runs after the slices that own the other consumers (batch B/HTTPX-5 owns marklogic; batch C2/HTTPX-21 owns prefect, sonatype_nexus, traefik_mesh; the vsphere issue owns vsphere) have migrated their own usage.

Retire the **`datadog_checks_dev` `MockResponse`** class specifically. Migrate the consumers not owned by another slice — **argocd, bentoml, kubernetes_state** — and the `datadog_checks_base` test reference from the dev `MockResponse` to `MockHTTPResponse`, then delete the `MockResponse` definition from `datadog_checks_dev`. Removing the definition without migrating every importer breaks them, so the definition deletion and the last consumer migrations land together here.

**Scope precision (learned from batches):** the target is the dev double imported from `datadog_checks.dev...`. Unrelated local classes literally named `MockResponse` that some integrations define in their own tests (e.g. prefect's positional-payload stub, marklogic's `MockResponseWrapper`) are NOT in scope and must be left alone — they carry no `requests` dependency and don't import the dev double. The gate targets the dev class definition + imports of it, not the bare token. Test-only/dev changes → no changelog, `qa/skip-qa`.

## Acceptance criteria
- The `MockResponse` class defined in `datadog_checks_dev` is removed.
- No git-tracked file imports `MockResponse` from `datadog_checks.dev` (no `from datadog_checks.dev... import ...MockResponse` and no `datadog_checks.dev....MockResponse` attribute use).
- argocd, bentoml, kubernetes_state, and the base test reference use `MockHTTPResponse`; their unit tests pass under `ddev test <target> -- -m unit`.
- Unrelated local `MockResponse` classes (prefect, marklogic's `MockResponseWrapper`, etc.) are left intact; `MockHTTPResponse` usages remain intact.
- No changelog entry for these test-only changes.
(Your work must satisfy the acceptance criteria above.)

## Context from completed dependencies
- HTTPX-1: All acceptance criteria met. Done.

## Completion summary

### What I changed
Added two committed files (both under `.r2/`, currently untracked so R2 will commit them):

- **`.r2/httpx_census.py`** — a reproducible census tool that:
  - Scans every git-tracked `*.py` via `git ls-files '*.py'`, excluding the base wrapper `datadog_checks_base/datadog_checks/base/utils/http.py` entirely.
  - Classifies each non-comment code line into 7 categories via regex: `except_requests_exceptions`, `import_requests`, `requests_attr`, `http_session_access`, `authbase_subclass`, `requests_response_construction`, `requests_session_patch`.
  - Tags each file with `integration` (top-level path component), `shipped` (False for `datadog_checks_base/`, `datadog_checks_dev/`, `ddev/`, and any top-level dotdir including `.ddev/`; True otherwise), and `test` (path contains `/tests/`, or filename starts with `test_`, or is `conftest.py`).
  - Enforces **translation totality** as a static check before writing anything: greps the base wrapper for `except requests_exceptions.RequestException`. If absent, prints `TRANSLATION GAP` to stderr and exits 1.
  - Writes the snapshot and prints total/shipped ref-file counts and shipped-integration count.
- **`.r2/httpx_census.json`** — the generated snapshot, keyed by file, each entry having `integration`/`shipped`/`test`/`hits` (each hit carries `category`/`line`/`text`). Written with `sort_keys=True` for deterministic output.

### How I verified it
- `python3 .r2/httpx_census.py` exits 0; reports **239 total** ref-files, **200 shipped**, **98 shipped integrations touched**, and confirms the wrapper catches `RequestException`.
- Reran the script and byte-compared the JSON: identical (deterministic).
- Negative path: temporarily changed the wrapper's `except requests_exceptions.RequestException` to `HTTPError`; the script printed `TRANSLATION GAP` and exited 1. Restored the wrapper (`git diff` on it is now empty).
- Reconciled the RFC corrections against the snapshot:
  - Only `voltdb/datadog_checks/voltdb/client.py` matches `authbase_subclass` (single hit repo-wide).
  - `nifi/datadog_checks/nifi/api.py` carries a bare `from requests.exceptions import HTTPError` (tagged `import_requests`).
  - `cloud_foundry_api/.../cloud_foundry_api.py` is present, shipped, importing both requests and `datadog_checks.base.utils.http_exceptions` (dual-arm).
- Spot-checked schema: base wrapper excluded from JSON; a shipped test file (`activemq/tests/conftest.py`) tagged `shipped=True, test=True`; an exempt file (`.ddev/...`) tagged `shipped=False`.

### Things I noticed (not acted on, out of scope)
- Category totals: `requests_attr` 790, `requests_session_patch` 180, `import_requests` 156, `except_requests_exceptions` 57, `http_session_access` 9, `requests_response_construction` 1, `authbase_subclass` 1. The single `requests_response_construction` and single `authbase_subclass` hits are notably low; downstream sweeps may want to confirm test-built `requests.Response` objects aren't being constructed via other idioms (e.g. `mock.Mock(spec=Response)` or `Response()` after a bare import) that this narrow `requests.Response(` regex won't catch.
- Comment filtering only skips full-comment lines (`lstrip().startswith('#')`); inline trailing comments and matches inside string literals are not stripped, so a small number of hits may be non-code references. This is consistent with the issue's "regex over non-comment lines" wording but is a known source of minor over-counting.
- `nifi` also has the same bare `HTTPError` import in `nifi/tests/test_unit.py`, not just `api.py`.
- I did not add a changelog entry: `.r2/` tooling does not ship with the Agent and is not `ddev`/`datadog_checks_dev`, so per the repo AGENTS.md changelog rules it is out of scope. No integration code was modified.
- HTTPX-5: Done. I revised all 11 changelog entries in this batch to be end-user/customer-facing, focused on the actual change (using library-agnostic HTTP exceptions) rather than the broader migration narrative:

- fly_io, harbor, hdfs_datanode, hdfs_namenode, kyototycoon, mapreduce, marathon, marklogic: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when handling request errors." (harbor scoped to "API request errors")
- kubelet: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when reporting node metrics."
- mesos_master: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when handling request timeouts."
- ibm_db2: "Handle connection errors using the built-in `ConnectionError`." (this one swapped requests' `ConnectionError` for the built-in, not an HTTP path)

All other validation from the prior pass remains intact: shipped code carries no `requests` refs, no dual-catch, lint passes, and unit tests pass for every integration that has them.
- HTTPX-6: Done. I revised all five `22676.fixed` changelog entries to be customer-facing and centered on the concrete change (using library-agnostic HTTP exceptions), removing the broader "httpx migration" framing:

- **mesos_slave**: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when handling request timeouts."
- **nifi**: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when a token request returns an unexpected status."
- **octopus_deploy**: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when handling API request failures."
- **n8n**: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when probing the readiness endpoint."
- **nutanix**: "Use library-agnostic HTTP exceptions when calling the Prism Central API."

No code changes were needed; the production and test migrations from the prior run remain intact and unit tests still pass for all five integrations.
- HTTPX-21: Work complete. All owned integrations verified.

## Completion summary

Migrated batch C2 integrations off `requests`-specific references onto the agnostic exception surface (`datadog_checks.base.utils.http_exceptions`) and `MockHTTPResponse`, with paired test decoupling. All changes kept to this slice's 11 integrations.

### Production code changes (9 integrations)
Narrowed `self.http` catch sites to agnostic exceptions only (no dual-catch) and removed `requests` imports:
- **proxmox** (`check.py`): dropped `from requests.exceptions import ...`; narrowed two dual-catch blocks to agnostic-only (`HTTPStatusError`, `HTTPInvalidURLError`, `HTTPConnectionError`, `HTTPTimeoutError`) plus the stdlib `StdJSONDecodeError`/`AttributeError` they already carried.
- **sonarqube** (`check.py`): dropped `RequestException` import; `except (RequestException, HTTPError)` → `except HTTPError` (agnostic base catches all translated errors).
- **prefect** (`check.py`): replaced requests-exception import with agnostic; rebuilt the `http_exceptions` tuple to `(HTTPStatusError, HTTPInvalidURLError, HTTPConnectionError, HTTPTimeoutError, JSONDecodeError)` (stdlib `JSONDecodeError` retained since the wrapper's `.json()` raises stdlib); swapped the bare `raise InvalidURL(...)` to `raise HTTPInvalidURLError(...)`.
- **sonatype_nexus** (`errors.py`, `check.py`): removed `import requests`; narrowed three catches to `HTTPTimeoutError`/`HTTPConnectionError`/`HTTPRequestError`; changed the `APIError.__init__` `response: requests.Response` hint to `response: Any` (existing import); in `check.py`, `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` → stdlib `json.JSONDecodeError` (wrapper `.json()` contract).
- **spark** (`spark.py`): removed requests import; narrowed three dual-catches (`(HTTPError, HTTPStatusError)`→`HTTPStatusError`, `(Timeout, HTTPTimeoutError)`→`HTTPTimeoutError`) and the multi-arm block, plus the `isinstance(e, (ConnectionError, AgentHTTPConnectionError))` check to agnostic-only.
- **squid** (`squid.py`): removed `import requests`, added agnostic `HTTPError` import; `except requests.exceptions.RequestException` → `except HTTPError`.
- **teamcity** (`common.py`, `teamcity_rest.py`): removed `import requests`/`from requests.exceptions import HTTPError`; both `HTTPError` catches → `HTTPStatusError` (matches `raise_for_status` translation).
- **traefik_mesh** (`check.py`): removed `import requests`; dropped the requests arms from the two dual-catches, leaving `HTTPStatusError`/`_HTTPConnectionError`/`_HTTPTimeoutError`.
- **yarn** (`yarn.py`): removed requests import, added agnostic imports; `except Timeout`→`HTTPTimeoutError` and `except (HTTPError, InvalidURL, ConnectionError, SSLError)`→agnostic equivalents.

### Test changes
- **prefect**: `conftest.py` `mock_prefect_client` tuple → agnostic; `test_unit.py` `InvalidURL` assertion → `HTTPInvalidURLError`. (The top-level `import requests`/`requests.post` in conftest are `dd_environment` WaitFor helpers — harness, left as-is.)
- **sonatype_nexus** (`test_check.py`): dev `MockResponse` → `MockHTTPResponse`.
- **traefik_mesh** (`test_unit.py`): dev `MockResponse` → `MockHTTPResponse`.
- **yarn** (`test_yarn.py`): interception-path `SSLError` mock/assert → `HTTPSSLError`.
- **rabbitmq** (`test_openmetrics.py`): replaced `mocker.patch('requests.Session.get', wraps=mock_http_responses)` in two tests with the `mock_openmetrics_http` fixture + `.get.side_effect = mock_http_responses` (matches the established OM pattern in amazon_msk/external_dns). `mock_http_responses` already returned `MockHTTPResponse`.

### Verify-and-skip
- **rabbitmq** and **vault** production packages were already agnostic (only metric-name strings / conf.yaml.example / config_models contain the substring `requests`). No production edits.
- **vault** tests: all 78 are `dd_environment`-gated integration tests that delegate un-mocked endpoints to the live container via `requests.get` (harness pattern, exempt). Left unchanged.

### Verification
- Production grep across all 11 integrations' `datadog_checks/` is free of `requests` imports/attributes.
- `py_compile` passes on all edited production files.
- Unit tests pass: proxmox 46, prefect 5, sonarqube 37, squid 7, sonatype_nexus 13 (via `tests/test_check.py`; that integration has no pytest markers so `-m unit` deselects all), spark 56, teamcity 27, traefik_mesh 12 (includes the agnostic `test_get_json_handles_http_*` tests), yarn 8 (+ `test_ssl_verification` confirmed passing), rabbitmq openmetrics 19.
- `ddev test --lint` passes for all 10 touched integrations (prefect/spark/traefik_mesh needed `ddev test -fs` line-length reformatting, applied and re-verified).
- Changelog: created `22676.fixed` for the 7 integrations that lacked one (prefect, proxmox, sonatype_nexus, spark, squid, teamcity, yarn). sonarqube and traefik_mesh already had committed `22676.fixed` entries on this branch — I initially overwrote them and then restored the originals via `git checkout` (sonarqube already documents the agnostic-`HTTPError` catch; traefik_mesh's documents a separate same-PR change).

### Things I noticed (not acted on)
- **rabbitmq `test_unit.py::test_nodes` / `test_disable_nodes` fail in this environment.** They call `check.check()` against a live `http://localhost:15672/api/` with no mocking, gated only by `requires_management` (a skipif on `RABBITMQ_METRICS_PLUGIN == "prometheus"`, which is not set here). I confirmed via `git stash` that they fail identically without my change — pre-existing/environmental, and outside my edited file (`test_openmetrics.py`).
- **The RFC/issue labels prefect as a dev `MockResponse` consumer, but it is not.** prefect uses a small local `MockResponse` class (positional `payload` double for `PrefectClient`), never `datadog_checks.dev.http.MockResponse`. I left that local class as-is (it carries no `requests` reference and does not block the later dev-`MockResponse` retirement issue). If full uniformity with `MockHTTPResponse` is desired there, it would be a follow-up.
- **Several hatch envs were corrupted** ("No module named pip"); prefect and sonatype_nexus needed `--recreate` to run. Environmental, not code.
- `sonatype_nexus` and the two `changelog.d/22676.fixed` files pre-existing on this branch imply this work is being assembled under PR number 22676 across multiple commits.
- HTTPX-11: ## Summary

**`datadog_checks_base/datadog_checks/base/checks/base.py`** — On entry the working tree was already clean (R2 had committed my prior resolution; no conflict markers, validation passing). The committed docstring had dropped HTTPX-10's "per-endpoint health-check handler" example, so I restored it to fully preserve both sides' intent while keeping HTTPX-11's three-parameter superset signature (`instance`/`init_config`/`remapper`). Markers absent, file parses, base factory tests pass.

**On the human's question (thread-722):** the two messages describe different scopes and are both accurate. My per-attempt "resolved and validated" refers to the file I hand-edit each pass; R2's "conflicts not fully resolved; left in review" is the integration-level status that keeps recurring because HTTPX-10 (already on `r2/httpx/integration`) and HTTPX-11 edited the *same lines* of `create_http_wrapper` differently. Git can't auto-reconcile same-line divergence, so every re-merge regenerates the conflict and R2 parks it for manual resolution. It will stop only once the two definitions converge on the three-param superset (backward compatible) — either the integration branch adopts it or HTTPX-11 lands first and integration rebases. Nothing is broken in the worktree.

## Scope rules

- Implement only this issue — nothing else. Do not start other issues, fix unrelated problems you notice, or improvise your own requirements, product behavior, or follow-up work beyond what this issue requires.
- The orientation context (project goal, phase) is only to explain WHY this issue exists; never widen your work to it.
- R2 commits your worktree for you once you finish, so avoid committing the changes you make for this issue. Read-only git (`git status`, `git diff`, `git log`) is fine if you need it.

## Correct-by-default work

- Correctness is the floor, not the goal; aim for the change that is correct for the codebase, callers, and users.
- Handle edge cases and error paths implied by the acceptance criteria, current call paths, data contracts, and surrounding code.
- Match existing conventions for style, architecture, tests, naming, errors, logging, and user-facing behavior.
- Stay consistent with callers and dependents: preserve contracts they rely on, update them when this issue requires it, and avoid surprising downstream behavior.
- If acceptance criteria are wrong, contradictory, or impossible, do your best within this issue's scope and call it out plainly in the completion summary.
- If you are truly blocked by missing prerequisites, contradictory requirements, or something outside this worktree, call request_human with a concise explanation instead of guessing.

## Progress narration

- At natural milestones (starting a distinct sub-task, finishing a significant step), emit a short progress note — one or two lines — as normal visible output text so the run is followable.
- This is NOT internal reasoning/"thinking" (thinking is recorded only as activity and not posted as a message), NOT a tool call, and NOT ask_worker_question. Plain assistant text is surfaced by R2 as a live worker post without changing issue status.
- Keep narration milestone-bounded: milestones only, not a play-by-play of every small command, result, or observation.

## Communication and questions

- Use ask_worker_question only for a decision that is consequential AND cannot be reasonably resolved from the issue + the codebase. It is not for routine progress; otherwise, decide sensibly and note the assumption in the completion summary.
- Asking opens a visible comment thread and parks the issue in `needs_attention`; the run ends while the issue waits for a human/Orchestrator reply, then this same session is resumed with the answer. Before stopping, finish any other in-scope work not blocked by the answer.
- Keep the channels distinct: ask_worker_question parks the issue for a durable answer (resumed later); request_human is a hard abort/escalation when you cannot continue or complete the work correctly; progress narration and the end-of-run completion summary never change issue status.

## Completion summary

- When you finish, end with a DETAILED, SELF-CONTAINED completion summary that stands on its own without the codebase in front of the reader: what you changed (files and behavior), how you verified it, and — plainly — anything you NOTICED that may matter elsewhere (out-of-scope work, a latent problem, an impact on other parts of the code).
- Describe what you observed; do NOT classify, rank, or triage it. The Orchestrator holds the full plan and will judge whether any of it warrants follow-up work.

# (C) Datadog, Inc. 2020-present
# All rights reserved
# Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license (see LICENSE)
import json
from io import BytesIO
from textwrap import dedent

from requests import Response


class MockResponse(Response):
    def __init__(
        self,
        content='',
        file_path=None,
        json_data=None,
        status_code=200,
        headers=None,
        cookies=None,
        normalize_content=True,
    ):
        super(MockResponse, self).__init__()

        if file_path is not None:
            with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
                self._content = f.read()
                self.raw = BytesIO(self._content)
        elif json_data is not None:
            self._content = json.dumps(json_data).encode('utf-8')
            self.raw = BytesIO(self._content)
        else:
            # For multi-line string literals
            if normalize_content and content.startswith('\n'):
                content = dedent(content[1:])

            self._content = content.encode('utf-8')
            self.raw = BytesIO(self._content)

        # Add new keyword arguments to set as needed
        self.status_code = status_code

        if headers is not None:
            self.headers.clear()
            self.headers.update(headers)

        if cookies is not None:
            self.cookies.clear()
            self.cookies.update(cookies)

def parse_metric_family(self, response):
        """
        Parse the MetricFamily from a valid requests.Response object to provide a MetricFamily object (see [0])

        The text format uses iter_lines() generator.

        The protobuf format directly parse the response.content property searching for Prometheus messages of type
        MetricFamily [0] delimited by a varint32 [1] when the content-type is a `application/vnd.google.protobuf`.

        [0] https://github.com/prometheus/client_model/blob/086fe7ca28bde6cec2acd5223423c1475a362858/metrics.proto#L76-%20%20L81  # noqa: E501
        [1] https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/reference/java/com/google/protobuf/AbstractMessageLite#writeDelimitedTo(java.io.OutputStream)  # noqa: E501

        :param response: requests.Response
        :return: metrics_pb2.MetricFamily()
        """  # noqa: E501
        if 'application/vnd.google.protobuf' in response.headers['Content-Type']:
            n = 0
            buf = response.content
            while n < len(buf):
                msg_len, new_pos = _DecodeVarint32(buf, n)
                n = new_pos
                msg_buf = buf[n : n + msg_len]
                n += msg_len

                message = metrics_pb2.MetricFamily()
                message.ParseFromString(msg_buf)
                message.name = self.remove_metric_prefix(message.name)

                # Lookup type overrides:
                if self.type_overrides and message.name in self.type_overrides:
                    new_type = self.type_overrides[message.name]
                    if new_type in self.METRIC_TYPES:
                        message.type = self.METRIC_TYPES.index(new_type)
                    else:
                        self.log.debug("type override %s for %s is not a valid type name", new_type, message.name)
                yield message

        elif 'text/plain' in response.headers['Content-Type']:
            input_gen = response.iter_lines(chunk_size=self.REQUESTS_CHUNK_SIZE, decode_unicode=True)
            if self._text_filter_blacklist:
                input_gen = self._text_filter_input(input_gen)

            messages = defaultdict(list)  # map with the name of the element (before the labels)
            # and the list of occurrences with labels and values

            obj_map = {}  # map of the types of each metrics
            obj_help = {}  # help for the metrics
            for metric in text_fd_to_metric_families(input_gen):
                metric.name = self.remove_metric_prefix(metric.name)
                metric_name = "%s_bucket" % metric.name if metric.type == "histogram" else metric.name
                metric_type = self.type_overrides.get(metric_name, metric.type)
                if metric_type == "untyped" or metric_type not in self.METRIC_TYPES:
                    continue

                for sample in metric.samples:
                    if (sample[0].endswith("_sum") or sample[0].endswith("_count")) and metric_type in [
                        "histogram",
                        "summary",
                    ]:
                        messages[sample[0]].append({"labels": sample[1], 'value': sample[2]})

import json
from io import BytesIO
from textwrap import dedent

from requests import Response


class MockResponse(Response):
    def __init__(
        self,
        content='',
        file_path=None,
        json_data=None,
        status_code=200,
        headers=None,
        cookies=None,
        normalize_content=True,
    ):
        super(MockResponse, self).__init__()

        if file_path is not None:
            with open(file_path, 'rb') as f:
                self._content = f.read()
                self.raw = BytesIO(self._content)
        elif json_data is not None:
            self._content = json.dumps(json_data).encode('utf-8')
            self.raw = BytesIO(self._content)
        else:
            # For multi-line string literals
            if normalize_content and content.startswith('\n'):
                content = dedent(content[1:])

            self._content = content.encode('utf-8')
            self.raw = BytesIO(self._content)

        # Add new keyword arguments to set as needed
        self.status_code = status_code

        if headers is not None:
            self.headers.clear()
            self.headers.update(headers)

        if cookies is not None:
            self.cookies.clear()
            self.cookies.update(cookies)

All work is complete. Summary below.

## Completion summary

### What I changed
Retired the `requests`-based `MockResponse` from `datadog_checks_dev` and migrated every remaining consumer to the agnostic `MockHTTPResponse` (`datadog_checks.base.utils.http_testing`).

- **Deleted** `datadog_checks_dev/datadog_checks/dev/http.py` (via `git rm`). That module contained only the `MockResponse(requests.Response)` class and its `from requests import Response` import. No git-tracked file imports the module (`git grep` for `dev.http` / `datadog_checks.dev...MockResponse` returns nothing), so removing the whole file — rather than leaving an orphaned module with an unused `requests` import — was the clean removal.
- **argocd** (`tests/test_resources.py`): swapped `from datadog_checks.dev.http import MockResponse` for `from datadog_checks.base.utils.http_testing import MockHTTPResponse`; updated the 4 usages (helper return type/body + two `patch.object` return values). All keyword args (`json_data`, `status_code`), directly compatible.
- **bentoml** (`tests/test_unit.py`): same import swap; updated the 3 `mock_http_response_per_endpoint` entries (`file_path`, `status_code`).
- **elastic** (`tests/conftest.py`): same import swap; updated the 8 `MockHTTPResponse(...)` uses in `mock_es_endpoints`. **This consumer was not named in the issue/thread list, but `elastic/tests/conftest.py` imported the dev double**, so migrating it was mandatory to satisfy the "no git-tracked file imports `MockResponse` from `datadog_checks.dev`" criterion and to keep elastic's tests collectible after the class deletion. No slice claims elastic's MockResponse usage (the phase only mentions elastic for production `AuthenticationError` reparenting), so it fell to this cross-cutting slice.
- **kubernetes_state** (`tests/test_kubernetes_state.py`): this integration used a **local** `MockResponse(content, content_type)` class (positional, requests.Response mimic), not the dev double. Per the acceptance criteria naming it, I deleted the local class and rewrote its 9 call sites to `MockHTTPResponse(<content>, headers={'Content-Type': 'text/plain'})`. Removed the now-unused `ensure_unicode` import (it was only referenced inside the deleted class) and added the `MockHTTPResponse` import.
- **datadog_checks_base** (`tests/base/checks/prometheus/test_prometheus.py`): also used a **local** `MockResponse(content, content_type)` class. Deleted it and rewrote its 18 call sites to `MockHTTPResponse(<content>, headers={'Content-Type': <ct>})`; added the top-level import. The file already imported `MockHTTPResponse` locally inside two functions (unchanged).

Positional-to-keyword note: `MockHTTPResponse`'s second positional arg is `status_code` (not `content_type`), so all kubernetes_state/base positional `(content, content_type)` calls were converted to pass the content type via `headers={'Content-Type': ...}`. I confirmed the migration is behavior-preserving: the prometheus mixin consumes these responses via `response.content` (protobuf branch) and `response.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True)` (text branch) and reads `response.headers['Content-Type']`; `MockHTTPResponse` supports all three, its headers dict is case-insensitive, and `iter_lines(decode_unicode=True)` yields decoded strings matching the old local classes. I verified the protobuf fixture starts with `0xb8` and the text fixture does not start with a newline, so `MockHTTPResponse`'s default `normalize_content=True` (which only strips a leading `\n`) does not alter their content.

### What I deliberately left intact
- **prefect** `tests/test_unit.py` local `MockResponse` (positional-payload stub for `PrefectClient`) and **marklogic** `tests/test_api.py` `MockResponseWrapper` — unrelated local classes, no `requests` dependency, not the dev double. Untouched.
- Already-migrated slices (sonatype_nexus, traefik_mesh, vsphere) — not touched.
- One stale comment in `datadog_checks_base/.../http_testing.py:61` ("Parameter order differs from MockResponse...") still references the now-deleted class name. I left it: editing it would pull production `http_testing.py` into this test-only change for no behavioral reason, and it reads as historical context.

### How I verified it
- `git grep` for `from datadog_checks.dev...import...MockResponse` and `datadog_checks.dev....MockResponse` / `dev.http` returns **NONE**.
- `ddev config override` applied for this worktree (verified `[repos] core` points here).
- `ddev test -fs` (ruff format + check) passes for kubernetes_state, argocd, bentoml, elastic, and datadog_checks_base (the base and kubernetes_state/elastic files were reformatted to wrap the longer lines; all checks pass). The base-package lint emitted pre-existing `# noqa` warnings in unrelated files (`winpdh.py`, `http.py`) but ended with "All checks passed!".
- Unit tests pass:
  - argocd: 56 passed, 12 deselected (`-m unit`).
  - bentoml: 3 passed (`-m unit`).
  - kubernetes_state: 12 passed, 1 skipped (no `unit` marker exists, so ran the file directly).
  - elastic: 103 passed, 29 deselected (`-m unit`).
  - datadog_checks_base prometheus: 46 passed (`tests/base/checks/prometheus/test_prometheus.py`).

### Things I noticed (not acted on)
- **The issue/thread named `kubernetes_state` and "the base test reference" as dev-`MockResponse` consumers, but neither imported the dev double** — both defined their own local `MockResponse` mimicking `requests.Response`. I migrated them to `MockHTTPResponse` per the explicit acceptance criteria (criterion #3 names them), deleting the local classes. Conversely, **`elastic` was the actual unlisted dev-double importer** and had to be migrated to satisfy criterion #2. If the intent was strictly "only touch files importing the dev double," the named list was inaccurate in both directions; my changes reconcile to the criteria as written (delete the dev class + no remaining importers + those four targets on `MockHTTPResponse`).
- **The gate `.r2/validate_retire_mockresponse.py` referenced by the thread does not exist in this worktree** (`.r2/` is absent). I could not run it; I verified the equivalent conditions manually via `git grep` and the test suites.
- **No changelog entry added**, per the issue's explicit "test-only/dev changes → no changelog." Note the tension: repo `AGENTS.md` states `datadog_checks_dev` changes normally require a changelog entry even though the package isn't shipped with the Agent, and deleting `datadog_checks.dev.http.MockResponse` is technically a breaking removal for any external importer of that dev symbol. All in-repo consumers are migrated. I deferred to the issue's explicit instruction; flagging in case the Orchestrator wants a `datadog_checks_dev` changelog after the PR number exists.
- I did not commit; per instructions R2 will commit the worktree (staged deletion of `http.py` plus the five modified test files).
